meta.name: India
meta.office: Office of the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)

drawings.colorUsageNote: Colour drawings are accepted only when absolutely essential for understanding; otherwise black-and-white line drawings in black ink are required under Rule 15.

procedural.gracePeriodNotes: India has no general grace period; only limited exceptions (e.g., Section 31 exhibitions and learned society disclosures) where specific conditions must be satisfied.

sequenceListing.lateFurnishingNotes: Sequence listing for nucleotide and amino acid sequences must be furnished in XML format compliant with WIPO Standard ST.26; text (TXT) or PDF may be filed for reference where permitted.

constraints: Limit to 1–3 sentences.
State the general and specific technical field without describing advantages or embodiments.

constraints: Do not exceed 15 words.
Avoid trademarks, personal names and the word 'patent'.
title.instruction: Draft a concise technical title for the Indian complete specification (Form 2).

constraints: Use 'comprising' as the preferred open connector; 'including' may also be used. Closed language such as 'consisting of' may be used when a narrow scope is intended.
Prefer a two-part format (preamble + 'characterised in that' / 'wherein') when defining improvements over known art.
Multiple dependent claims are permitted, including those dependent on other multiple dependent claims, but dependencies must be clear.
All claims must be fully supported by the detailed description and comply with unity of invention under Section 10(5).

constraints: Provide a high-level overview of the inventive concept and key distinguishing features.

Avoid claim-style language and marketing or speculative statements.
Describe relevant prior art and technical problems addressed by the invention.
Avoid unnecessary admissions that particular documents are prior art unless intentional.
Do not state that the invention is obvious, trivial or a mere workshop modification.

variants.label: Indian Complete Specification (Form 2)

ui.helpText: Title must indicate the specific features of the invention and is normally in not more than 15 words, as per Rule 13(7)(a) of the Patents Rules, 2003. No strict character limit exists in the Rules; focus on brevity and clarity, avoiding trademarks, personal names and the word 'patent'.
ui.placeholder: Enter a concise technical title (≤15 words).

ui.helpText: 1–3 sentences indicating the technical field to which the invention relates; usually the opening part of the description as per the Manual of Patent Office Practice and Procedure.
ui.placeholder: State the technical field of the invention.

ui.helpText: Briefly describe the prior art and the technical problem addressed. Avoid unnecessary admissions that specific documents are prior art and avoid statements implying the invention is obvious or a mere workshop modification.
ui.placeholder: Describe prior art and problems addressed by the invention.

ui.helpText: Provide a concise summary of the invention highlighting the essential features and distinguishing aspects over the known art, consistent with the independent claims. Recommended by the Manual to precede the detailed description for clarity.
ui.placeholder: Provide a concise summary or statement of the invention.

ui.helpText: List each drawing figure with a one-line description (e.g., “FIG. 1 is a schematic view of a system configuration”). Ensure figure numbering and captions match the drawing sheets filed under Rule 15.
ui.placeholder: List each figure briefly (e.g., “FIG. 1 is a schematic view of …”).

ui.helpText: Describe the invention in full detail, including best mode, so that a person skilled in the art can perform the invention, as required by Section 10(4) of the Patents Act, 1970. Refer consistently to reference numerals in the drawings and provide sufficient embodiments to support all claims.
ui.placeholder: Provide a full detailed description with reference to drawings where applicable.

ui.helpText: Claims must define the matter for which protection is sought and be supported by the description as per Section 10(4) and (5). Use clear, precise language; maintain unity of invention; and draft each claim as a single sentence with proper dependencies.
ui.placeholder: Write independent and dependent claims in separate numbered paragraphs.

ui.helpText: Provide a concise summary in not more than 150 words, commencing with the title of the invention and indicating the technical field, technical advancement and principal use of the invention, in line with Section 10(4)(d) and Rule 13(7)(b). Avoid claim-style wording.
ui.placeholder: Short technical abstract (≤150 words) starting with the title.

variants.description: Complete specification for a patent application filed using Form 2 under the Patents Act, 1970 and Patents Rules, 2003.

title.message: Title appears long; Indian practice under Rule 13(7)(a) expects a specific title normally in not more than 15 words.

claims.message: Number of claims exceeds 10; additional official fees apply for each claim beyond ten under the First Schedule. Keep claim set succinct and consistent with unity of invention under Section 10(5).

abstract.message: Abstract exceeds the 150 word limit under Rule 13(7)(b).

crossSectionChecks.message: Some claim elements may not be clearly supported in the detailed description as required by Section 10(4).
Abstract may describe features not clearly reflected in the independent claims; ensure consistency with the claimed subject-matter.

checkList.description: Ensure all reference numerals in drawings are defined in the detailed description.
Ensure key claimed elements appear in at least one figure when drawings are present.

notes: No hard legal limit on the number of claims. However, larger claim sets increase official fees and examiner workload.
Unity of invention under Article 37 requires that the claims relate to a single general inventive concept.
Multiple dependent claims may refer to two or more preceding claims, but must not depend on other multiple dependent claims.

feeModel.note: Examination and annuity fees are calculated based on the total number of claims; there is no separate fee based purely on the number of independent claims.

notes: No formal maximum page limit in JPO practice; keep the description as concise as compatible with full enablement. Many JP specifications fall in the 20–50 page range.
All substantive content must be in Japanese for grant; English may be used at filing under foreign-language application rules with later Japanese translation.
Use horizontal (left-to-right) text only for the description, with approximately 40 characters per line and not more than 50 lines per page. Do not use vertical text.

The abstract should provide a brief summary of the disclosure. There is no explicit statutory word or character limit, but keeping it concise (~100–150 English words or roughly ≤800 characters) is consistent with JPO and JAPIO publication practice.
Avoid statements of advantages, commercial value, or speculative effects.
Drawings must be suitable for black-and-white reproduction. Standard practice is to use black line drawings, solid lines, and hatching.

Grayscale or photographic images (e.g., micrographs, spectrograms, complex images that cannot be represented adequately by conventional line drawings) are acceptable only where necessary and must still reproduce clearly in black-and-white.
Reference numerals should be used consistently and correspond to those in the description.
The granted patent will be in Japanese. English may be used at filing in a foreign-language written application, with a Japanese translation due within the statutory period under Article 36-2.

For PCT national phase entry in Japan, a Japanese translation of the international application must be filed in accordance with national phase timing rules (typically by the national entry date, with special windows in certain late-entry situations).
Translations of foreign-language applications are assessed for added matter; ensure that the Japanese translation does not introduce subject matter beyond the content of the original application.
Description and drawings must be on A4 paper with margins of at least 2 cm on all sides; left and right margins should not exceed 2.3 cm.
Use easily readable fonts and ensure text contrast suitable for black-and-white reproduction.
The 40-characters-per-line guidance is strict for Japanese text; English lines may naturally vary around this value as long as margins and readability are preserved.

Japan provides a 12-month grace period under Article 30 for certain disclosures by the applicant or related persons (including specified exhibitions and cases of abuse), subject to proper declaration and conditions.

To invoke the grace period, a request/declaration must be made at filing and a proving document for the disclosed invention is, in principle, to be submitted within 30 days from the filing date (or national-phase entry), in accordance with Article 30(2) practice.

The grace period applies only to enumerated acts under Article 30(1), such as disclosures by the applicant or right holder, specified exhibitions, or disclosures against the will of the applicant (abuse).
Japan does not use a US-style Information Disclosure Statement (IDS) system; however, applicants should avoid deliberate concealment of known prior art.
allowProvisionalPriorityNotes: Priority may be claimed from applications that qualify as a regular national filing under the Paris Convention, including provisional applications or provisional specifications in systems such as the US, UK, and Australia, provided they adequately disclose the invention.

notes: The description must be clear and sufficient for a person skilled in the art to carry out the invention (Article 36(4)(i)).
At least one embodiment considered the best mode should be described as part of the embodiments section, consistent with JPO drafting practice.
Chemical formulas, mathematical formulas, and tables must fit within 170 mm × 255 mm and cannot extend across pages.
Use portrait orientation (A4 ‘height long’) only; landscape orientation for formulas or tables is not permitted.
If nucleotide or amino acid sequences are disclosed, a sequence listing compliant with WIPO ST.26 in XML format is required.
Late furnishing is possible under certain conditions but may involve additional procedures or fees.

spec_pdf.label: Specification (JP-compliant PDF)
notes: This export is intended to approximate a JPO-compliant filing format, including paragraph numbers in the 〖0001〗 style.
Optional sections such as Citation List, Industrial Applicability, and Reference Signs List should be omitted automatically from the final export if they are empty.
Ensure all section headings follow the recommended labels (e.g., 〖Technical Field〗, 〖Background Art〗) when translated into Japanese or formatted for filing.

prompts.systemPrompt: You are an expert Japanese patent drafter assisting in the preparation of a Japanese patent specification in compliance with the Japan Patent Office (JPO) practice. Follow the section structure, rules, and validation constraints given in the JSON configuration. Use neutral, technical language, ensure enablement for a person skilled in the art, and maintain consistency across sections.

title.content: Generate a concise technical title for the invention. Avoid marketing language, trade names, and personal names. The title should briefly identify the invention’s technical subject matter.

claims.content: Draft the Claims defining the scope of protection. Prepare at least one independent claim capturing the essential features, and dependent claims that add further limitations. Ensure each claim is supported by the description, uses clear and consistent terminology, and maintains unity of invention (a single general inventive concept).

abstract.content: Draft a single-paragraph Abstract summarizing the gist of the invention and its main technical features. Keep it concise (ideally around 150 words, not exceeding 200 words). Avoid advantages, speculative effects, marketing language, and references to specific claims.

citation_list.content: If prior art references are available, list them in a Citation List section, separating patent literature and non-patent literature. Use consistent citation style and avoid commentary; this section is optional and should be omitted if no references are being cited.

background_art.content: Draft the Background Art section. Describe conventional technology and prior art relevant to understanding the invention. Identify drawbacks or limitations in objective terms, without disparaging specific prior patents by name. Do not fully state the technical problem, solution, or advantageous effects of the invention here; reserve those for the Summary of Invention.

technical_field.content: Draft the Technical Field section. State clearly the technical field to which the invention pertains, using neutral technical terminology and avoiding discussion of advantages or problems.

reference_signs_list.content: Optionally draft a Reference Signs List, listing each reference numeral and its corresponding element name for clarity, especially where numerous numerals appear in the drawings.

summary_of_invention.content: Draft the Summary of Invention. Present the technical problem arising from the Background Art, the solution provided by the invention, and any advantageous effects in a clear problem–solution structure. Use language aligned with the independent claims and avoid excessive promotional tone.

industrial_applicability.content: Optionally draft the Industrial Applicability section, explaining how the invention can be used in industry where this is not obvious. If applicability is self-evident, this section may be omitted or kept very brief.

description_of_embodiments.content: Draft the Description of Embodiments, providing one or more embodiments in sufficient detail to enable a person skilled in the art to carry out the invention without undue experimentation. Include the embodiment regarded as the best mode. Use consistent reference numerals corresponding to the drawings and avoid drafting in pure claim-style language.

brief_description_of_drawings.content: Draft the Brief Description of Drawings. For each figure, provide a one-sentence description (e.g., "FIG. 1 is a block diagram of..."). Do not include detailed operation or advantages; reserve such details for the Description of Embodiments section.

diagramGenerationHints.block: Use rectangles for hardware or software components and arrows for signal, data, or control flow. Labels should match reference numerals used in the Description of Embodiments.
diagramGenerationHints.graph: Use clear axes labels, units, and legends. Graphs must remain readable when reproduced in black-and-white.
diagramGenerationHints.table: Use tables for parameter lists, experimental results, or configuration options. Tables must fit within the allowed A4 printable area and not span multiple pages.
diagramGenerationHints.flowchart: Use standard flowchart symbols for processes and decisions. Ensure the flow corresponds to the claimed method steps and that step numbering matches the description.
diagramGenerationHints.schematic: Use simplified circuit or system symbols showing functional relationships. Keep linework clean for black-and-white reproduction and match labels to the claims and description.
diagramGenerationHints.cross_section: Use cross-sectional views to clarify internal structure. Hatching must be used to distinguish different materials or components where appropriate.
diagramGenerationHints.exploded_view: Use exploded views to show assembly relationships between components. Ensure each part is assigned a unique reference numeral referenced in the Description of Embodiments.
diagramGenerationHints.perspective_view: Use perspective or isometric views to show overall apparatus structure. Ensure all important structural elements are labeled with reference numerals.

variants.label: Standard Patent Application

ui.helpText: Provide a short technical title identifying the invention. Avoid marketing language, trade names, personal names or overly broad phrases.
ui.placeholder: Enter a concise title of the invention

ui.helpText: This section is optional and may be used to mention priority or related applications where not already fully captured in the request form. Keep it brief and factual.
ui.placeholder: Indicate priority/related applications, if needed

notes: In JPO practice, priority and related application data are primarily handled in the Request form. Use this section only if additional explanatory context is necessary in the specification.

ui.helpText: State the technical field to which the invention pertains. Use neutral, technical language and, where appropriate, include classification terms or general field names.
ui.placeholder: Describe the technical field of the invention

ui.helpText: Explain conventional technology and prior art relevant to understanding the invention. Describe drawbacks or limitations in objective terms, but avoid criticizing specific prior patents by name. Do not fully state the invention’s technical problem, solution, or advantages here—reserve those for the Summary of Invention.
ui.placeholder: Describe background art and conventional techniques

ui.helpText: Optional section to list prior art references. Separate patent literature and non-patent literature where possible, using consistent citation style. Include this section only if you actually have prior art references to list.
ui.placeholder: List patent and non-patent literature, if desired

ui.helpText: State the technical problem to be solved, the solution provided by the invention, and any advantageous effects, in a clear problem–solution structure. Keep language aligned with the claims and avoid excessive marketing language.
ui.placeholder: Summarize the invention in terms of problem and solution

ui.helpText: For each drawing figure, provide a one-sentence description (e.g., "FIG. 1 is a block diagram of..."). Do not describe details that belong in the embodiments.
ui.placeholder: Briefly describe each figure (if drawings are provided)

ui.helpText: Describe one or more embodiments in sufficient detail to enable a person skilled in the art to carry out the invention without undue experimentation. Include the embodiment considered the best mode. Use reference numerals consistently with the drawings.
ui.placeholder: Provide a detailed description of embodiments of the invention

ui.helpText: This section is optional. Describe how the invention can be used in industry where such applicability is not self-evident from the other sections.
ui.placeholder: Explain industrial applicability if not obvious

ui.helpText: Optional section listing each reference numeral and its corresponding element name for clarity, especially when many numerals are used in the drawings.
ui.placeholder: List reference numerals and their meanings

ui.helpText: Draft one or more claims supported by the description. Use clear, consistent terminology. Independent claims should define the essential features. Dependent claims may further limit the invention.
ui.placeholder: Draft claims defining the scope of protection

ui.helpText: Write a concise summary of the invention. Typically one paragraph, stating the gist of the invention and its main technical features. Avoid advantages, marketing language, and references to claims.
ui.placeholder: Provide a brief summary of the invention

variants.description: Standard Japanese patent application under the Patent Act (Law No. 121 of 1959, as amended) and associated Enforcement Regulations.

title.message: Title is unusually long. Consider shortening to a concise technical expression.

abstract.message: Abstract exceeds 200 words. JPO practice prefers concise abstracts (~150 words).
Abstract exceeds ~800 characters. JPO/JAPIO publication practice favors concise abstracts and longer texts may be shortened on publication.

crossSectionChecks.message: Key elements in the independent claims are not reflected in the Summary of Invention. Consider aligning the summary with the claimed problem–solution.
Claims appear broader than the embodiments disclosed. Ensure that each claimed feature is enabled by the Description of Embodiments.
The Abstract does not accurately reflect the main embodiment(s) described. Adjust for consistency.
Claims may relate to multiple general inventive concepts. Check unity of invention under Article 37 and consider divisional strategy.

One or more figure numbers or reference signs in Brief Description of Drawings do not appear or are not explained in the Description of Embodiments. Ensure every figure and reference sign used in the drawings is supported and described.

checkList.description: Ensure all figure numbers and reference numerals mentioned in the Brief Description of Drawings are defined and explained in the Description of Embodiments.
Ensure that key claimed structural elements appear in at least one drawing figure when drawings are present, and that figure references can be traced via the Brief Description of Drawings.

sequenceListing.notes: For international applications filed on or after 1 July 2022 that disclose nucleotide or amino acid sequences, a sequence listing complying with WIPO Standard ST.26 in XML format is required. Depending on how and when the listing is furnished (for example, as a missing part of the description under PCT Rule 20.5), late submission may lead to correction of the international filing date or to limitations on the international search.

designatedStates.notes: Under PCT Rule 4.9(a), filing a PCT application automatically designates all PCT Contracting States. Elections for the purposes of international preliminary examination under Chapter II are made later via the demand, which generally results in the election of all previously designated States.

sectionHeadings.detailed_description: MODE(S) FOR CARRYING OUT THE INVENTION

constraints: Limit to 1–3 sentences.
State the technical field without describing advantages or embodiments.
Ensure the field statement would make sense on its own to a skilled person.
field.instruction: Draft the Technical Field section for the PCT description.

constraints: Do not exceed 500 characters.
Do not use trademarks or the word 'patent'.
title.instruction: Draft a concise technical title for the PCT international application.

constraints: Use 'comprising' as the preferred open-ended connector unless a closed or alternative formulation is justified.
Avoid two-part 'characterized in that' format unless specifically requested for a designated Office.
Each dependent claim must refer back to a single previous claim and not to another multiple dependent claim.
All claim features must be supported by the description of the mode(s) for carrying out the invention.
claims.instruction: Draft a complete set of claims compliant with PCT Rule 6 and Rule 13.

constraints: Explain the invention as defined in the claims so that the technical problem and its solution can be understood.
Mention advantageous effects of the invention compared with the background art, where appropriate.
Ensure consistency with the independent claims.
summary.instruction: Draft the Disclosure of Invention section.

constraints: Preferably 50–150 words; do not exceed 150 words.
Summarize the technical disclosure so that the field, problem, solution and principal use can be understood.
Do not include statements on alleged merits, advantages or speculative applications.
Do not use claim-style language.
abstract.instruction: Draft the Abstract for the PCT application.

constraints: Describe relevant prior art and its limitations, preferably citing representative documents where appropriate.
Avoid explicit admissions that specific references are prior art unless clearly intended.
Do not state that the invention is obvious or trivial.
Describe embodiments in sufficient detail that a skilled person can carry out the invention without undue experimentation.
Set out at least the best mode contemplated by the applicant.
Ensure that each independent claim has at least one fully supporting embodiment.
Reference the drawings and reference numerals where applicable.
detailed_description.instruction: Draft the section describing the mode(s) for carrying out the invention.

diagramGenerationHints.block: Use rectangles for components and arrows for data/control flow.
diagramGenerationHints.flowchart: Use standard flowchart symbols for processes and decisions.
diagramGenerationHints.schematic: Show functional relationships and reference numerals clearly.
diagramGenerationHints.perspective_view: Use where overall physical form is important.

variants.label: Standard PCT International Application

ui.helpText: Enter a short, specific technical title. Do not include trademarks or the word 'patent'.
ui.placeholder: Enter a concise technical title

ui.helpText: Use this section for priority and benefit claims (for example, earlier national, regional or international applications) and related applications, consistent with PCT Rule 4.10.
ui.placeholder: Identify related applications and priority claims, if any.

ui.helpText: 1–3 sentences specifying the technical field as required by PCT Rule 5.1(a)(i); do not describe advantages or embodiments here.
ui.placeholder: State the technical field to which the invention relates.

ui.helpText: Indicate background art useful for understanding, searching and examining the invention, preferably citing documents, as per PCT Rule 5.1(a)(ii). Avoid statements that the invention is obvious.
ui.placeholder: Describe the background art and its limitations.

ui.helpText: Explain the invention as claimed so that the technical problem and its solution can be understood, and state advantageous effects, if any, with reference to the background art, as per PCT Rule 5.1(a)(iii).
ui.placeholder: Provide a high-level disclosure of the invention as claimed.

ui.helpText: One sentence per figure, briefly describing what is shown, to satisfy PCT Rule 5.1(a)(iv).
ui.placeholder: Briefly describe each figure of the drawings.

ui.helpText: Set forth at least the best mode contemplated by the applicant for carrying out the invention, using examples and referring to the drawings where appropriate, as per PCT Rule 5.1(a)(v). The description must be sufficiently clear and complete for a person skilled in the art.
ui.placeholder: Describe at least one way of carrying out the invention in full detail.

sections.label: Mode(s) for Carrying Out the Invention

ui.helpText: Present the claims in the clearest possible form, each as a single sentence where practicable, numbered consecutively, and drafted in accordance with PCT Rule 6.
ui.placeholder: Independent and dependent claims for the international application.

ui.helpText: Summarize the disclosure so the technical field, problem, solution and principal use can be understood, preferably in 50–150 words, without statements on alleged merits or speculative applications, as per PCT Rule 8.1.
ui.placeholder: Concise technical abstract (preferably 50–150 words).

variants.description: International patent application filed under the Patent Cooperation Treaty for the international phase before entering national or regional phases.

title.message: Title appears long; consider making it more concise for clarity in the international phase.
Title exceeds the 500 character maximum typically permitted for PCT titles.

claims.message: Number of claims exceeds the recommended 20; a large claim set may increase fees or complexity before some receiving Offices, International Authorities and designated Offices.

abstract.message: Abstract exceeds 150 words; PCT practice recommends an abstract of about 50–150 words.

crossSectionChecks.message: Some claim elements may not be clearly supported in the description of the mode(s) for carrying out the invention.
Abstract may describe features not clearly reflected in the independent claims.

checkList.description: Ensure all reference numerals in drawings are explained in the description.
Ensure key claimed elements appear in at least one figure when drawings are present.

sequenceListing.lateFurnishingNotes: Required for nucleotide and amino acid sequence listings as per USPTO adoption of WIPO ST.26.

constraints: Limit to 1-3 sentences.
State the technical field without describing advantages or embodiments.
field.instruction: Draft the Field of the Invention section.

constraints: Do not exceed 500 characters.
Do not use trademarks or the word 'patent'.
title.instruction: Draft a concise technical title for the US patent application.

constraints: Use 'comprising' as the preferred connector.
Avoid two-part 'characterized in that' format.
Each dependent claim must reference a single prior claim.
Claims must be supported by the detailed description.
claims.instruction: Draft a full set of claims compliant with US patent practice.

constraints: Provide a high-level overview of the inventive concept.
Ensure consistency with the independent claims.
summary.instruction: Draft the Summary of the Invention section.

constraints: No more than 150 words.
Summarize the technical disclosure without mentioning advantages or benefits.
Do not use claim-style language.
Describe relevant prior art and its limitations.
Avoid explicit admissions that specific references are prior art unless clearly signaled.
Do not state that the invention is obvious or trivial.
background.instruction: Draft the Background of the Invention section.

constraints: Describe embodiments in sufficient detail to practice the invention.
Ensure each independent claim has at least one supporting embodiment.
Reference drawings where applicable.

diagramGenerationHints.block: Use rectangles for components and arrows for data or control flow; keep labeling consistent with the detailed description.
diagramGenerationHints.flowchart: Use standard flowchart symbols for processes and decisions; ensure the flow reflects the claimed method steps.

variants.label: Standard US Utility Application

ui.helpText: Must be short and specific; no trademarks, no word 'patent'. Max 500 characters as per 37 CFR 1.72(a).
ui.placeholder: Enter a concise technical title

ui.helpText: Include application number and filing date; use for priority/benefit claims and related applications as per 37 CFR 1.78.
ui.placeholder: Identify related US or foreign applications, if any.

ui.helpText: 1-3 sentences indicating the technical field; avoid advantages or embodiments here.
ui.placeholder: State the technical field of the invention.

ui.helpText: Explain limitations of known techniques without conceding obviousness.
ui.placeholder: Describe prior art and problems.

ui.helpText: Outline the invention and key aspects; may mention advantages as per MPEP summary guidance, but keep consistent with the claims.
ui.placeholder: Provide a high-level summary of the invention.

ui.helpText: One line per figure, for example: 'Fig. 1 is a schematic view of a system configuration.'
ui.placeholder: List each figure briefly.

ui.helpText: Describe embodiments in sufficient detail to enable practice of the invention and satisfy 35 USC 112; reference the drawings where appropriate.
ui.placeholder: Provide a full detailed description.

ui.helpText: Each claim as a separate numbered paragraph, compliant with 35 USC 112 and 37 CFR 1.75.
ui.placeholder: Independent and dependent claims.

ui.helpText: Up to 150 words, technical only; no advantages or marketing.
ui.placeholder: Short technical abstract.

variants.description: Regular non-provisional utility application under 35 USC 111(a) or national phase under 35 USC 371.

title.message: Title appears long; USPTO prefers short, specific titles.
Title exceeds the 500 character maximum under 37 CFR 1.72(a).

claims.message: Number of claims exceeds recommended 20; may increase examination costs.

crossSectionChecks.message: Some claim elements may not be clearly supported in the detailed description.
Abstract may describe features not clearly reflected in the independent claims.

checkList.description: Ensure all reference numerals in drawings are defined in the detailed description.
Ensure key claimed elements appear in at least one figure when drawings are present.
